summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Defended hearing – Where it is just and equitable to adjust the parties’ property interests – Where the husband failed to provide full and frank financial disclosure – Where the financial circumstances of the husband’s corporations cannot be independently verified – Where the husband’s personal income is not independently verified – Where the husband was the primary breadwinner – Where the wife was the primary carer of the children and homemaker – Where the wife has continued to provide primary care for the children since separation – Where the wife has borne the primary financial responsibility for the children since separation – Where no nexus exists between the wife’s admitted assault on the husband and his ability to make contributions – Where the husband perpetrated coercive and controlling family violence – Where contributions assessed at 57.5/42.5 with a 20 per cent adjustment in favour of the wife – Orders made – Wife to retain the proceeds of sale of the former matrimonial home – Superannuation splitting order made from the husband’s super.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

AUSTIN J:

These reasons explain the determination of financial proceedings between the parties brought under Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”).

SHORT HISTORY

The parties married in 2005 and finally separated in mid-2022.
The wife commenced these proceedings in March 2023.
The husband paid the wife $40,000 by way of “partial property settlement” from property he sold pursuant to an interim consent order made in April 2023. Then, pursuant to another consent order made in October 2024, the wife received another $150,000 as an “interim property” distribution from the net proceeds realised on the sale of the jointly owned former family home. The wife has partly spent the interim payments. The residue funds are in her bank accounts.
Pursuant to final parenting orders made consensually between the parties in July 2025, the parties’ two children live predominantly with the wife and spend time with the husband.
Due to the husband’s failure to comply with procedural directions, the trial fixed in November 2025 was vacated on his application and he was ordered to pay the wife’s costs thrown away by the adjournment. The trial was instead fixed in February 2026 and proceeded at that time.

PROPOSALS

The wife began the trial seeking the orders set out within her Second Further Amended Initiating Application filed on 15 September 2025. She sought the entirety of the remaining funds realised on the sale of the former family home, one-half of the husband’s superannuation interest, and the parties’ retention of the personal property in their respective possession. By the time of final submissions, she sought even more of the available property.
The husband initially sought the orders set out within his Amended Response filed on 25 November 2025. He sought equal division of the remaining funds realised on the sale of the former family home, including the interim property adjustment paid to the wife, and subject also to her sharing equally a tax liability incurred by him. Otherwise, he wanted equal division of their household contents, equal division of pre-separation superannuation interests, and equal liability for pre-separation debts. However, by final submissions, the husband accepted the wife was entitled to a greater share of the known property than his proposed orders provided.

EVIDENCE

The wife relied upon:
(a) her affidavit filed on 22 October 2025;

(b) her reply affidavit filed on 8 December 2025; and

(c) her financial statement filed on 7 November 2025, which superseded the earlier one filed on 22 October 2025.

The husband relied upon:
(a) his affidavit filed on 25 November 2025; and

(b) his financial statement filed on 25 November 2025.

The husband was refused leave to adduce in evidence a second affidavit sworn by him on the first day of trial, which had only been given to the wife’s lawyers a few minutes before the trial started. No procedural order was made for him to file any supplementary affidavit but, in any event, he had no satisfactory explanation for its lateness or how the prejudice to the wife caused by such late service of the evidence could be satisfactorily cured. The wife objected to only the admissibility of the text of the affidavit, acknowledging the husband would be free to tender as exhibits (subject to admissibility) any documents referred to in the affidavit.
The father next made an application to suspend the operation of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) under s 102ND(3)(b) of the Act to try and belatedly save the admissibility of his affidavit filed in November 2025 but, once that application failed, the mother successfully made some objections to parts of that affidavit.
Conformably with r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), the parties agreed the annexures or exhibits to their affidavits should not be regarded as forming part of the evidence until such documents were separately tendered as exhibits. Relatively few were.[1]

LEGAL PRINCIPLES

The trial of the financial cause began after 10 June 2025, in which event the amendments to the Act wrought by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) now apply.
In accordance with the new provisions of Pt VIII of the Act, it is first necessary to establish the existing property interests and liabilities of the parties (s 79(3)(a)), which then illuminates whether it is just and equitable to make any property adjustment order at all (s 79(2)).
If a property adjustment order is warranted, when deciding what order to make, the Court must consider the parties’ respective contributions (s 79(3)(b)(i) and s 79(4)) and their current and future circumstances (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)).
There are no companion animals to consider here (ss 79(1), 79(6) and 79(7)).

EXISTING PROPERTY INTERESTS

Wife

Ultimately, the parties agreed the wife’s existing assets, liabilities and superannuation interests are as follows:
Assets

The wife’s liability for the family loan raised to pay legal fees is disregarded in the adjustment exercise. That is a debt for which she should bear exclusive responsibility (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)(e)). She did not contend otherwise. Her divisible property therefore has an increased net value of $392,121.

Husband

The husband failed to comply with his obligation of full financial disclosure (s 71B), just as the wife alleged in her affidavit,[2] though his counsel had puzzling difficulty conceding at the start of the trial whether the extent of his financial disclosure was incomplete, late, or both. It was both, as the husband’s counsel was obliged to accept during final submissions.
Over the weekend just passed, the husband furnished to the wife, purportedly in fulfilment of his duty of disclosure:
(a) an unfiled and unsigned personal tax return for the financial year ended June 2022;[3]

(b) an unsigned financial statement for G Pty Ltd for the financial year ended June 2023;[4] and

(c) unsigned financial statements for E Pty Ltd for the financial years ended June 2022 and June 2023.[5]

The husband was impelled to concede in cross-examination there were inconsistencies within the documents which he could not explain. For example, the husband’s draft 2022 tax return disclosed his receipt of an unfranked dividend of $230,000 from E Pty Ltd as income over and above his salary in that financial year, which dividend was not reflected in the 2022 financial records of E Pty Ltd. The unsigned documents could at best be deemed to be in “draft” format and therefore attract very little probative weight. They are not yet official records for either him or the corporations.
Even allowing for the draft documents produced to the wife within the last few days, the husband breached his express obligation (s 71B(9); r 6.06(8) of the Rules) by failing to disclose:
(a) his three most recent taxation returns;

(b) his three most recent taxation assessments;

(c) the three most recent financial statements and the last four business activity statements for the two Australian corporations he controls, being E Pty Ltd and G Pty Ltd (though the latter was not incorporated until late 2022)[6]; and

(d) the three most recent financial statements and the last four business activity statements for the American corporation he controls, being E LLC.

The upshot is the husband has not produced any document to officially verify the financial circumstances of his corporations since the parties separated years ago.
Nor is his personal income independently verified. He deposed to earning $1,296 per week in the financial statement filed in April 2023 but deposed to income of only $62 per week in the financial statement filed in November 2025. The wife had good reason to be suspicious about the reliability of such evidence about his income when not corroborated by taxation returns, PAYG statements and the like.
The historical bank account records the husband did disclose to the wife nearly two years ago do indeed reveal deposits and withdrawals, but the transactions are unexplained.[7] The duty of disclosure relates to all relevant “information and documents” – not just documents (s 71B(1)). The statutory duty envisages that the party also disclose relevant information, which duty is not fulfilled simply by delivering a jumble of bank records to the opposing party. Relevant “information” includes an explanation for the significant and otherwise unexplained deposits and withdrawals in bank account records.
Aside from the husband’s breach of statutory duty, he has breached Court orders. By procedural orders made on 21 July 2025, the husband was directed to update and disclose the financial records of E Pty Ltd and G Pty Ltd for the financial years commencing from July 2021 and July 2023 respectively, but he did not do so.[8] Such orders only affirmed the husband’s prudential obligations under federal corporate and revenue legislation. The husband sought to say, or at least imply, he could not afford to bring the corporate financial records up to date,[9] but that is not an adequate excuse for his prudential failure for two reasons: first, his failure has extended over three years of this litigation when he has been possessed of substantial funds; and secondly, his separate statutory duty as sole director demanded compliance regardless.
The husband admitted in cross-examination that E Pty Ltd had about $450,000 in its bank accounts at about or shortly after the time of separation (being a bank balance of $261,000 in June 2022 plus two payments totalling about $195,000 it received from H Pty Ltd in July 2022), yet the funds were all but exhausted only 18 months later in December 2023. Save for the sum of about $220,000 spent on an office fit-out for G Pty Ltd during 2023, the husband did not explain the expenditure of the residue $230,000.
The husband claimed in cross-examination his shares in E Pty Ltd and E LLC were worthless, but his lay opinion evidence is unreliable. There is no expert opinion evidence to that effect.
The husband’s lay opinion evidence about the value of his shares in G Pty Ltd is also unreliable. He estimated the value at $150,000 in his financial statement,[10] yet claimed the value to be $220,000 in his Case Outline document. When asked to explain the discrepancy in cross-examination he was unable to do so comprehensibly. He evidently estimated the value of the shares by reference to the cost of the corporate office fit-out, but that evidence only raised more questions than it answered. Why would the husband spend $220,000 (as stated in his Case Outline document) on an office fit-out for G Pty Ltd when he alleges being paid gross income of only $62 per week for his employment by that corporation?[11] Why would G Pty Ltd have rented premises at a cost of over $5,000 per month since January 2023?[12] The husband was keen to emphasise his business acumen, so it is unlikely he would have spent such capital pointlessly. His evidence of G Pty Ltd’s current lack of liquidity and his own meagre income from that corporation is not very convincing. Regardless, his counsel accepted G Pty Ltd should be valued at $220,000 as an admission against his interest.
Attention may then turn from the husband’s assets to his alleged liabilities.
The husband claimed he incurred a taxation liability by the sale of cryptocurrency,[13] but the proposition is rejected. He alleged he sold the cryptocurrency at a loss,[14] so no capital gains tax could possibly have been incurred on the sale. The husband’s counsel agreed the liability is not proven.
The husband claimed to be indebted for income tax for financial years preceding and up to 2021,[15] but the debt is not the subject of any other evidence in his affidavit.[16] No official documents to verify the liability were produced. This tax liability cannot be the tax allegedly incurred on the abovementioned sale of the cryptocurrency because that sale did not occur until May 2023.[17] The alleged historical tax debt is not proven. The husband’s counsel agreed.
The husband claimed to be indebted for accountancy fees,[18] though the alleged debt was not included in his financial statement.[19] No invoice was produced to verify the debt. The alleged debt is not proven. The husband’s counsel agreed.
The husband alleged his liability for various loans advanced by three different creditors: J Company; Mr K; and Mr L.[20]
The J Company loan is not the husband’s personal loan. If it is truly a loan, then the proper debtor is G Pty Ltd, which the husband conceded by affirming the funds advanced were a “business loan” for which G Pty Ltd is liable.[21] In cross-examination, when confronted with bank records, the husband admitted G Pty Ltd paid a total of about $113,000 to J Company in September 2023 and May 2025, which facts he did not mention in his evidence-in-chief. The husband confirmed he could not prove any other financial transactions with J Company by resort to any other document. The alleged J Company loan is not proven. The husband’s counsel agreed.
The loan from Mr K is not a personal loan. Assuming it is truly a loan, then the proper debtor is G Pty Ltd, which the husband effectively confirmed in cross-examination when he admitted that all but a few hundred dollars of all funds advanced were used to pay the lease for G Pty Ltd. He also admitted in his Case Outline document that the funds were used for “[G Pty Ltd] operating costs”. Those admissions collided irreconcilably with the husband’s assertion in his financial statement that the loan was for “living expenses”.[22] The alleged loan from Mr K is not proven. The husband’s counsel agreed.
The loan from Mr L was supposedly for the husband’s “legal and living expenses”,[23] but in cross-examination the husband admitted the loan was for his “legal fees primarily”, though he was unable to estimate the proportionality. It is impossible to reconcile that evidence with the husband’s contention in his Case Outline document that the funds were advanced in January 2024 and were used for “business expenses”. Even if the funds were used personally by the husband to pay legal fees, as with the wife, that is a liability for which he should bear exclusive responsibility (s 79(3)(b)(ii) and s 79(5)(e)). The husband’s counsel agreed.
The husband claims his present liability for a total of about $335,330 in credit card debt,[24] which is much greater credit card debt than existed at the time of the parties’ separation in around June 2022. In cross-examination, the husband conceded the total credit card debt at the time of separation approximated $70,000. He gave no evidence at all about how the extra debt of some $265,330 was incurred between the time of separation and trial.[25] The wife contended the increase in credit card debt incurred by the husband from the time of separation should be quarantined and borne exclusively by him, which proposition is accepted. If the husband’s counsel did not agree in final submissions, he had nothing more to say about it.
The wife, and ultimately also the husband, agreed that according to admissions made by him, his known assets, liabilities and superannuation interests comprise:

Excluding the loan for legal fees ($38,365) and the unexplained proportion of credit card debt incurred by the husband after separation ($335,330 – $70,000 = $265,330), for which he should be exclusively responsible, the husband’s known divisible property is valued at $746,153.
The known divisible assets and superannuation interests are therefore valued in aggregate at $1,138,274 (= $392,121 + $746,153).

IS IT JUST AND EQUITABLE TO MAKE ANY ORDER?

The parties agreed it would be just and equitable to make property adjustment orders, though they disagreed over the nature of such orders. It is just and equitable to adjust their property interests, not least because of the need to sever their joint legal entitlement to the residue trust funds realised on the sale of the former family home and, as they both sought, to split the husband’s superannuation interest.

CONTRIBUTIONS

When the parties married in 2005, neither had any property of any realisable net value. Neither party gave evidence of their accumulated superannuation entitlements at the time.[26]
In 2007, the parties jointly purchased a home in Region O of NSW, which purchase was facilitated by a loan advanced by the husband’s relative. Part of the loan, which the husband estimated to be $6,500, was later forgiven.[27] The rest of the loan was repaid.
The husband worked full-time throughout the parties’ relationship.[28]
The wife worked full-time until the birth of their first child in 2013.[29]
Given the husband’s evidence about his devotion to employment and business opportunities throughout the marriage,[30] his assertion of equivalently sharing household tasks with the wife during the marriage is rejected.[31] After the wife began bearing children, the husband was the primary breadwinner and the wife was the primary carer for the children and the household.[32]
In 2009, the wife received an inheritance of $30,000 which was spent within the household.[33]
After the parties separated in 2022, the wife carried on providing primary care to the children.[34] She also bore the primary financial burden of maintaining the children after separation.[35] She also struggled to service debt.[36]
The significant controversial factual issue between the parties in respect of their contributions concerned the commission of “family violence” and the extent to which it affected their respective “ability” to make contributions (s 79(4)(ca)).
The wife alleged the husband’s commission of family violence by coercive and controlling conduct related to his tight control of household finances, both during their relationship,[37] and after their separation, which included him blocking her use of a credit card when she had no other means of meeting essential household expenses like groceries and appliance repairs.[38] The husband conversely alleged the wife had unfettered access to the household finances,[39] which seems unlikely but, even if that is true, his evidence does not meet and refute the wife’s evidence of his restrictive surveillance of their financial position. The text messages between the parties tendered by the husband did not refute the wife’s evidence either.[40] The messages are just as consistent with the husband directing the wife about how to organise the household finances and her having to ask for money. The wife’s evidence of the husband’s financial control is accepted, despite her concession in cross-examination that the husband did sometimes give her money for personal expenditure. The fact she had to ask him for money to spend on herself tends to demonstrate how he controlled their finances.
There is no doubt the husband failed to comply with interlocutory orders made during the litigation, which failure caused the wife’s financial circumstances to be tightly constricted. He failed to rectify the mortgage arrears on the former family home when he consented to do so,[41] which required the wife to make two more interlocutory applications for financial relief.[42] Eventually, the wife had no option but to sell the home to avoid foreclosure on the mortgage.[43] Understandably, the wife was stressed and anxious over the security of accommodation for herself and the children from early 2023 until the home was eventually sold in early 2025.[44] Her evidence in that regard is accepted. She was not challenged about it.
The wife also alleged the husband has stalked her since their separation,[45] and deliberately damaged and removed property from the family home,[46] which incidents added to her stress.[47] The husband did not meet and refute such evidence in his own evidence-in-chief even though he had the wife’s first affidavit for about a month before he filed his own affidavit. She was challenged tangentially about some of the evidence in cross-examination, but only ineffectually, and so her evidence is accepted. The husband’s attempt to benignly explain his position in cross-examination is not accepted.
Inferentially, the wife’s performance of her role as primary carer for the children, both before and after separation, was likely impinged by the husband’s financial control, the worry he caused her about residential security, and the stress she experienced about her personal safety. Therefore, her “ability” to provide non-financial contributions was likely adversely affected.
The wife’s evidence of her snide disparagement by the husband to the children in her absence,[48] is not relevant for present purposes because, if that occurred, it is an adverse reflection upon his parenting capacity rather than a curtailment of the wife’s “ability” to make contributions. The wife’s counsel did not wish to be heard against that conclusion.
Conversely, the husband alleged the wife’s “volatile anger” caused him anxiety.[49] He also alleged she once assaulted him in 2016,[50] which she admitted. Notwithstanding, the husband’s evidence of his exemplary employment performance between the time of the marriage in 2005 and the separation in 2022 precludes any nexus being drawn between such “family violence” committed by the wife and his “ability” to make financial or non-financial contributions.
By virtue of evidentiary objections successfully taken to the husband’s evidence,[51] during final submissions the husband expressly abandoned his former allegations or imputations of:
(a) the curtailment of his employment opportunities due to his capitulation to the wife’s pressure to abandon his interest in such business opportunities;[52] and

(b) episodic misconduct he impliedly attributed to the wife.[53]

In final submissions, the wife contended her contribution-based entitlement should be assessed at 65 per cent, whereas the husband submitted the wife’s contribution-based entitlement should be assessed at 45 per cent.
Until separation, the parties contributed both financially and non-financially to the extent of their respective capacity in an effort to further the interests of their family. Overall, the wife’s contributions were comparatively greater on account of: her capital contribution of her inheritance in 2009; her sole care of the children in Australia in 2021 and 2022 while the husband lived in the USA; her primary care of the children since separation in 2022; and the financial and other forms of control the husband exerted over her throughout the marriage and after separation which hampered her ability to contribute. The wife’s contribution-based entitlement is assessed at 57.5 per cent.

ADJUSTMENT

Only some of the factors prescribed by s 79(5) of the Act were invoked by the parties.

Sections 79(5)(b) and 79(5)(c)

The husband is seven years older than the wife, but he is still only 51 years of age. He still has a long working future. Both are in good physical health, though the wife has deteriorated psychological health.[54]
The husband conceded he does have a greater income-earning capacity than the wife, but his earning capacity is substantially greater for several reasons: his greater historical income; the wife’s current income being derived from only welfare payments and manual labour;[55] and the wife will be the future primary care of the children (aged 12 and 10 years), one of whom has special needs.[56]
As the husband was keen to emphasise when discussing his financial contributions, his past income as long ago as 2016 was as much as USD $200,000 and his expertise took him around the world.[57] He admits that by the time the parties separated in 2022 he was self-employed and earning a more modest income. He voluntarily chose to pursue self-employed contract work over full-time employment about two years before separation.[58] As mentioned, the husband incorporated G Pty Ltd in late 2022 and then expended $220,000 on an office fit-out of premises commercially leased by G Pty Ltd, so it stands to reason he expects to recoup such capital expenditure by increased future income.
Only a few months ago, in November 2025, the husband participated as an interviewee in a podcast.[59] He held himself out as an authoritative and experienced speaker on the topic of the podcast. He was impelled to accept the exercise was intended to promote his profile and how during the interview he exhorted viewers to refer to the E Pty Ltd website for more information, which website he keeps current.
The husband now represents his income for 2022 at $284,169.[60] In the financial statement he filed in April 2023, he represented his income at $1,296 per week (or about $68,000 per annum). In the financial statement he filed in November 2025, he represented his income at $62 per week (or about $3,000 per annum). No sensible explanation is proffered by the husband for such a staggering deterioration in his income in the face of his past income-earning performance, his capital expenditure on G Pty Ltd, and his promotional work as recently as only a few months ago for E Pty Ltd. Such a dramatic decrease in income is not sufficiently explained by him simply observing it has decreased.

Section 79(5)(d)

The wife has had the benefit of interim payments amounting to $190,000,[61] but only $40,000 was paid by the husband from his property. The other $150,000 was paid from jointly-owned property, to which the husband contributed only one-half. However, the wife still has $77,523 saved in her bank accounts, so she has only spent about $110,000 of the total sum she received.
In the meantime, the husband comparatively had access to and used $450,000 from the coffers of E Pty Ltd. Some $220,000 of that expenditure is now accounted for in the value of G Pty Ltd, but the husband’s residual expenditure of $230,000 remains unexplained. His post-separation expenditure therefore depleted marital assets much more significantly than the wife’s expenditure.

Section 79(5)(e)

The parties’ exclusive responsibility for some of their own debts has already been addressed when identifying and valuing their individual assets and liabilities. That discussion need not be repeated.

Sections 79(5)(f)

By reason of the sale of the former family home, the wife and children now live in a flat annexed to the home of the maternal grandparents.[62] She needs cash to either buy or secure a long-term lease over alternate accommodation for them.

Sections 79(5)(i) and 79(5)(j)

The wife is presently entitled to welfare payments.[63]
The husband does not claim any entitlement to welfare payments.

Section 79(5)(s)

The husband pays no child support.[64]

Sections 79(5)(v)

As the wife submitted, the evidence of the husband’s derelict financial disclosure easily attracts the application of the principles distilled in authorities about not being unduly cautious in the generous allocation of known property to her (Black and Kellner [1992] FamCA 2; (1992) FLC 92-287 at 79,133–79,134; Weir and Weir (1993) FLC 92–338 at 79,592–79,595; Chang v Su [2002] FamCA 156; (2002) FLC 93-117 at [28]–[32], [57]–[72]).

Conclusion

The husband conceded an adjustment of five per cent in the wife’s favour was warranted, but a more generous adjustment is justified. The adjustment should be measured at 20 per cent, which equates to $227,655 on the known pool of net assets and superannuation. That sum is less than the income the husband asserts he earned in only one year – the financial year ended June 2022.[65] Being 51 years of age, the husband has at least 15 years of work ahead of him and plenty of time within which to financially recover using his asserted skill.
The wife is therefore entitled to 77.5 per cent of the known net assets and superannuation, which equates to property worth $882,162.
The husband’s 22.5 per cent share computes to $256,112.

DISPOSITION

Presently, the wife has control of assets and superannuation with a net value of $392,121.
To hold property with a net value of $882,162, the wife needs to receive extra property worth $490,041.
By the time of final submissions, the parties acknowledged the wife should receive extra property by way of a combination of cash and superannuation splitting order, but there was no clear consensus as to the proportionality.
The husband wanted to equalise the parties’ superannuation interests.[66] He evidently recognised his working future will enable him to accumulate much more superannuation than the wife. She also wanted additional superannuation by splitting the husband’s superannuation interest,[67] but not at the cost of a cash component which would enable to her to establish another independent household for herself and the children.[68]
If the wife receives the whole of the residue sale proceeds from the former family home, as she sought, she will derive $288,873 in cash from the husband. Aside from the shares he holds in the private corporations, which would be difficult to liquidate, he has no other asset which can be easily liquidated to pay cash to the wife. The remaining $201,168 (= $490,041 – $288,873) due to the wife must therefore come from the split of the husband’s superannuation interest, though the figure can be rounded down to $200,000.
The base amount of $200,000 is split from the husband’s superannuation interest, which will leave him with superannuation of about $102,000 and furnish the wife with about $236,000 in superannuation.
For that purpose, the husband’s superannuation interest held with Super Fund 1 is found and declared to be valued at $302,012 and the trustee of the fund is found and declared to have been afforded procedural fairness in respect of the superannuation splitting orders.[69]
The husband will remain liable for the unpaid costs order made against him when the original trial was vacated.[70]
The husband conceded two orders sought by him within his Amended Response could not be made.[71]
Such an outcome is just and equitable.
